coredata.dc:title: Biomass derived worm-like nitrogen-doped-carbon framework for trace determination of toxic heavy metal lead (II)
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Cuixing
Liu, Jingju
Bi, Yanni
Ma, Chongbo
Bai, Jing
Hu, Zongqian
Zhou, Ming
coredata.dc:description: The worm-like nitrogen-doped-carbon framework (WNCF) with abundant edge-plane-like defective sites (EDSs) was synthesized by using natural wax gourd (Benincasa hispida) as the main carbon precursor and milk yielded by Chinese Holstein cattle (Holstein Friesian) as the nitrogen precursor for the first time. The Nafion-dispersed WNCF (Nafion-WNCF) was employed to design a highly sensitive electrochemical sensor for the trace determination of toxic heavy metal lead (II) (Pb2+) by the differential pulse anodic stripping voltammetry (DPASV). Some key experimental factors including calcination temperature of WNCF, pH value of the buffer solution, deposition potential, deposition time and the concentration of bismuth (Bi3+) were optimized for the stripping analysis of Pb2+. Under the optimum experimental condition, Nafion-WNCF modified bismuth film glassy carbon electrode (Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE) exhibits a wide linear range from 0.5 μg L−1 to 100 μg L−1 and a low detection limit of 0.2 μg L−1 (S/N = 3) for detecting Pb2+. Especially, Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE was successfully applied to determine Pb2+ in tap water and lake water samples. All the results suggest that the WNCF can be considered as a green and low-cost nanomaterial for the precision detection of Pb2+ in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass
Nitrogen-doped-carbon framework
Bismuth film
Stripping voltammetry
Lead (II)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of worm-like nitrogen-doped-carbon framework (WNCF) from biomass
Fabrication of worm-like carbon framework (WCF) from biomass
Characterization of worm-like nitrogen-doped-carbon framework from biomass (WNCF) and worm-like carbon framework from biomass (WCF)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of WCF (A) and WNCF (B). (C) XRD patterns of WCF (a) and WNCF (b). (D) Raman spectra of WCF (c) and WNCF (d).
(A) XPS spectra of WCF (a) and WNCF (b). (B) N 1s spectrum of WNCF. (C) O 1s spectra of WCF and WNCF. (D) FT-IR spectra of WCF (c) and WNCF (d).

(A) CV curves of GCE (a1), Nafion/GCE (a2), Nafion-WCF/GCE (a3) and Nafion-WNCF/GCE (a4) in 10 mM K3Fe(CN)6 containing 0.1 M KCl. Scan rate: 10 mV s−1. (B) Nyquist plots of GCE (b1), Nafion/GCE (b2), Nafion-WCF/GCE (b3) and Nafion-WNCF/GCE (b4) in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.1 M KCl. Inset: the equivalent circuit. (C) DPASV curves for 50 μg L−1 Pb2+ at Nafion/BFGCE (c1), Nafion-WCF/BFGCE (c2) and Nafion-WNCF-BFGCE (c3) in 0.1 M pH 4.5 acetate buffer containing 1.4 μg mL−1 Bi3+. Deposition potential: −1.2 V. Deposition time: 360 s. Amplitude: 80 mV. Increment potential: 5 mV. Quiet time: 15 s.

Optimization for the experimental conditions. Effects of (A) calcination temperature during WNCF synthesis, (B) pH value of buffer solution, (C) deposition potential, (D) deposition time and (E) Bi3+ concentration on the DPASV responses of 50 μg L−1 Pb2+ at Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE. Other DPASV conditions are identical to Fig. 3C.

(A) DPASV curves of Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE in solution containing 1.4 μg mL−1 Bi3+ for the analysis of Pb2+ over a concentration range of 0.2–120 μg L−1. (B) Calibration plot for Pb2+ detection at Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE. (C) The DPASV curves of interfering metal ions containing 50 μg L−1 Pb2+ under the optimal conditions. (D) DPASV curves for 50 μg L−1 Pb2+ detection at five Nafion-WNCF/BFGCEs. (E) DPASV responses for Pb2+ detection in lake water by using standard addition method at Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE. (F) DPASV responses for Pb2+ detection in tap water using standard addition method at Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE. Other DPASV conditions are identical to Fig. 3C.
Schematic of the biomass derived WNCF for trace determination of toxic heavy metal Pb2+.
Analytical performance of different bismuth-based electrochemical sensors for Pb2+ detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: SWASV, square wave anodic stripping voltammetry; CNT, carbon nanotube; G, grapheme; MWCNTs, multi-walled carbon nanotubes; RGO, reduced graphene oxide; BiF4-CPE, carbon paste electrode bulk-modified with ammonium tetrafluorobismuthate; BiOCI, bismuth-oxychloride particle; IPA, isopropyl alcohol; C–Bi, carbon xerogels containing bismuth nanoparticles; CS, chitosan; NPs, nanoparticles; SPCPE, screen printed porous carbon electrode.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical performance for Pb2+ determination in different real samples.

head.ce:title: Biomass derived worm-like nitrogen-doped-carbon framework for trace determination of toxic heavy metal lead (II)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Nanobiosensing and Nanobioanalysis at Universities of Jilin Province, Key Laboratory of Polyoxometalate Science of Ministry of Education, National & Local United Engineering Laboratory for Power Batteries, Department of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun, Jilin Province, 130024, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The worm-like nitrogen-doped-carbon framework (WNCF) with abundant edge-plane-like defective sites (EDSs) was synthesized by using natural wax gourd (Benincasa hispida) as the main carbon precursor and milk yielded by Chinese Holstein cattle (Holstein Friesian) as the nitrogen precursor for the first time. The Nafion-dispersed WNCF (Nafion-WNCF) was employed to design a highly sensitive electrochemical sensor for the trace determination of toxic heavy metal lead (II) (Pb2+) by the differential pulse anodic stripping voltammetry (DPASV). Some key experimental factors including calcination temperature of WNCF, pH value of the buffer solution, deposition potential, deposition time and the concentration of bismuth (Bi3+) were optimized for the stripping analysis of Pb2+. Under the optimum experimental condition, Nafion-WNCF modified bismuth film glassy carbon electrode (Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE) exhibits a wide linear range from 0.5 μg L−1 to 100 μg L−1 and a low detection limit of 0.2 μg L−1 (S/N = 3) for detecting Pb2+. Especially, Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE was successfully applied to determine Pb2+ in tap water and lake water samples. All the results suggest that the WNCF can be considered as a green and low-cost nanomaterial for the precision detection of Pb2+ in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution caused by the discharge of heavy metal ions has drawn great attention owing to high toxicity, fast diffusibility and bio-accumulativity [1–5]. Among the ions, lead (II) (Pb2+) is one of the most hazardous ions in water, and is ranked in the list of “ten hazardous substances of public health concern” [6]. Pb2+ can accumulate in human body and cause serious nervous system and kidney injuries, resulting in physical discomfort, such as cancer, encephalopathy, irritability, psychosis and muscle paralysis [7,8], even at trace levels [8]. Accordingly, it is considerably significant to explore a sensitive analytical method for precisely monitoring the levels of Pb2+ in different media. Up to now, there are different developed analytical techniques for the detection of Pb2+, including atomic fluorescence spectrometry [9], surface-enhanced Raman spectrometry [10] and electrochemical method [11,12]. Of them, the electrochemical method is considered to be attractive in the quantification of Pb2+ because of high sensitivity, reliability, simplicity and rapidity [12,13]. In particular, anodic stripping voltammetry (ASV) has been widely regarded as a well-established tool for metal ions analysis due to its ability to perform accumulation of the metal ions and their further stripping off the electrode to generate current response associated with the target concentration [14,15].

The suitable electrode material is recognized as a crucial part for achieving highly sensitive detection [16]. In the past two decades, mercury film has been widely used for sensitive electrochemical sensors fabrication owing to its remarkable stripping properties [14]. Paradoxically, severe toxicity and environmental hazard of mercury restrict the continuous use of mercury film-based sensors for electroanalysis [17]. Recently, bismuth film has received considerable attention as a candidate for the fabrication of voltammetry sensors [18,19] due to its ability to form fused alloys with numerous metal ions as well as well-defined stripping behaviors, low toxicity of bismuth, excellent signal-to-background ratio and wide linear range [15,20]. The bismuth film electrodes with the improved electrochemical signals can be prepared by depositing on various electrode substrates like noble metals electrodes, screen printed electrodes, glassy carbon electrodes, graphene and carbon nanotubes modified electrodes et al. [20,21]. However, new electrode substrates are still required in construction of novel electrochemical sensing platform for sensitive detection of heavy metal ions.

Nature presents us with almost limitless source to fabricate artificial materials with novel chemical structures and outstanding properties [22]. Recently, the biomass derived carbon nanomaterials have attracted considerable interest due to low-cost, extensively available and renewable superiorities [23,24], which open up a feasible route to the low-cost development of carbon-based catalysts. Furthermore, doping heteroatoms (such as B, N, S and O) into the carbon materials can effectively regulate electronic properties of carbon materials for efficiently catalytic process [16]. Of these dopants, N atom is the first preference because of its matchable atomic size with C atom and five valence electrons for bonding the neighbouring C atom. The replacement of C by N atom in carbon materials can impact the electronic structure of original carbon frameworks and produce abundant active sites and structural defects, which allows the achievement of favorable electrochemistry [16].

As one of the fast-growing, ubiquitous and long-season vegetables, wax gourd (Benincasa hispida) is widely grown in most of the Asian countries [25]. Additionally, milk, as a popular beverage, is easy to obtain and rich in protein with abundant nitrogen elements. Herein, we prepared a worm-like nitrogen-doped-carbon framework (WNCF) by employing wax gourd and milk as raw materials for the construction of an electrochemical Pb2+ sensor, Scheme 1 . The nitrogen doping can endow WNCF with high density of edge-plane-like defective sites, which are helpful for the nucleation of lead and bismuth during the electrodeposition process and thus enhancing the stripping performance of Pb2+ [26–28]. Furthermore, the Pb2+ levels in tap water and lake water samples were precisely detected at Nafion-dispersed WNCF modified bismuth film glassy carbon electrode (Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE). In our system, Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE toward the detection of Pb2+ shows the merits of high sensitivity, wide linear range and low detection limit which are superior to some recently reported bismuth-based electrochemical Pb2+ sensors [29,30].

The wax gourd (Benincasa hispida) was obtained from the local supermarket in Changchun, P.R. China. The packaged milk with the brand of Gaogai which was yielded by Chinese Holstein cattle (Holstein Friesian) was purchased from Inner Mongolia Mengniu Dairy Co., Ltd (P.R. China). Nafion solution (5 wt% in a mixture of 15–20% lower aliphatic alcohols and water) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, and then 1 wt% Nafion solution was obtained by diluting it with isopropyl alcohol. KCl and NaCl were purchased from Aladdin (P.R. China). Al(NO3)3 .5H2O and Co(NO3)2 .6H2O were purchased from Tianjin Hongyan Chemical Reagent (P.R. China). CaCl2, MgCl2 .6H2O, MnSO4 .H2O, CuSO4 .5H2O, Zn(NO3)2 .6(H2O) and FeCl2 .4H2O were obtained from Tianjin Baishi Chemical Reagent (China). Ni(NO3)2, Hg(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)3 and Cd(NO3)2 were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (P.R. China). The stock aqueous solutions of lead (II) (Pb2+) (100 mg L−1) and bismuth ion (Bi3+) (100 mg L−1) were prepared from Pb(NO3)2·4H2O and Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, respectively, which were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). The frozen lake water was obtained from the Jing Lake in the campus of Northeast Normal University. After being kept in a sealed centrifuge tube at room temperature for 48 h, the melting lake water was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane (Millipore, USA). Then, the obtained lake water was stored at 4 °C for further use. The tap water obtained from a laboratory in Yifu Scientific Center of Northeast Normal University was stored at 4 °C for further use. All solutions were prepared with doubly distilled water unless otherwise indicated.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns and Raman spectra were performed on X-ray Diffractor D4 (Brucker, German) by employing Cu Kα radiation and a Renishaw Raman system model 1000 spectrometer, respectively. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were taken by Nicolet Magna 560 FT-IR spectrometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained from an ESCA LAB spectrometer (USA). ALPHA 1–2 LD plus (Germany) and Philips XL-30 ESEM were used to perform freeze-drying steps and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) measurements, respectively. The graphite furnace atomic absorption spectrometry (GF-AAS) data was obtained from SpectrAA-220Z (VARIAN, USA).

The CHI660E electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, P.R. China) was used to perform the electrochemical measurements such as differential pulse anodic stripping voltammetry (DPASV). A three-electrode cell consisting of Pt wire, glassy carbon electrode (GCE, d = 3 mm) and Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) as counter, working and reference electrodes, respectively, was used in these electrochemical measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of worm-like nitrogen-doped-carbon framework (WNCF) from biomass
ce:section.ce:para: First, the flesh of fresh wax gourd was cut into cubes with the size of ∼2 × ∼2 × ∼2 cm3. Then, we placed the mixture of ∼10 g of wax gourd and 20 mL of doubly distilled water into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Then, the autoclave was subject to a heating treatment at 180 °C for 10 h. After that, we washed thoroughly the black monolith product with doubly distilled water and then submerged it in 200 mL hot doubly distilled water (∼60 °C) for 48 h. After being freeze-dried at −44 °C for 24 h, the resultant aerogels were immersed into 100 mL of milk at room temperature for 1 h. After keeping the obtained product in oven at 70 °C in air atmosphere overnight and then in a tube furnace at 800 °C in N2 atmosphere for 2 h, the WNCF was obtained. We also fabricated WNCF-700 or WNCF-900 by changing the temperature in tube furnace to 700 °C or 900 °C during the synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of worm-like carbon framework (WCF) from biomass
ce:section.ce:para: First, the flesh of fresh wax gourd was cut into cubes with the size of ∼2 × ∼2 × ∼2 cm3. Then, we placed the mixture of ∼10 g of wax gourd and 20 mL of doubly distilled water into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Then, the autoclave was subject to a heating treatment at 180 °C for 10 h. After that, we washed thoroughly the black monolith product with doubly distilled water and then submerged it in 200 mL hot doubly distilled water (∼60 °C) for 48 h. Afterwards, the dry sample was obtained by freeze drying at −44 °C for 24 h. After being heated in oven at 70 °C in air atmosphere overnight and then being heated in a tube furnace at 800 °C in N2 atmosphere for 2 h, the WCF was obtained.

We carefully polished bare GCE with 0.05 μm alumina powder and rinsed it with distilled water before use. Then, the electrode was ultrasonic-cleaning sequentially in nitric acid, ethanol and distilled water for 1 min, respectively, and dried in air. In order to fabricate Nafion-dispersed WNCF modified GCE (Nafion-WNCF/GCE), we first prepared Nafion-WNCF suspension by ultrasonically dispersing 10 mg of ground WNCF powder in 2 mL of 1 wt% Nafion solution. After drop-casting 5 μL of Nafion-WNCF suspension on GCE surface and then being dried under an infrared lamp, Nafion-WNCF/GCE was obtained. For comparison, Nafion-dispersed WNCF-700 modified GCE (Nafion-WNCF-700/GCE) and Nafion-dispersed WNCF-900 modified GCE (Nafion-WNCF-900/GCE) were prepared by the similar method.

In order to fabricate Nafion-dispersed WCF modified GCE (Nafion-WCF/GCE), 10 mg of ground WCF powder was ultrasonically dispersed in 2 mL of 1 wt% Nafion solution to be Nafion-WCF suspension. After drop-casting 5 μL of Nafion-WNCF suspension on of GCE surface and then being dried under an infrared lamp, Nafion-WCF/GCE was obtained. In order to fabricate Nafion modified GCE (Nafion/GCE), 5 μL of 1 wt% Nafion solution was coated onto GCE surface, followed by drying under an infrared lamp.

0.1 M pH 4.5 acetate buffer solutions containing 1.4 μg mL−1 Bi3+ and known or unknown concentrations of Pb2+ were adopted as electrolyte to carry out differential pulse anodic stripping voltammetry (DPASV) tests. Under magnetic stirring condition, the preconcentration step took place at −1.2 V and was kept for 360 s to ensure simultaneous deposition of Bi3+ and Pb2+ on the surface of the working electrode. After stopping the agitation and waiting for 15 s, the voltammograms with the amplitude of 80 mV, a pulse period of 0.2 s, a potential step increment of 5 mV and potential window from −1.0 to −0.2 V were recorded. The previously unstripped Pb and Bi on the electrode surface were removed by a 150 s step at 0.4 V (with stirring) before the next analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of worm-like nitrogen-doped-carbon framework from biomass (WNCF) and worm-like carbon framework from biomass (WCF)
ce:section.ce:para:
The morphologies of the as-synthesized WNCF as well as the sample without nitrogen doping (i.e., WCF) were first characterized by the scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Fig. 1 A–B, WCF and WNCF both possess the worm-like nanostructure, indicating the nitrogen doping process with the help of green reagent of milk did not destroy the worm-like framework nanostructure. In Fig. 1C, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of WCF (a) and WNCF (b) reveal two main diffraction peaks centered at ∼22° _ 25° and ∼44°, which represented the diffractions of (002) and (100) planes from hexagonal graphite, respectively [31]. However, the (002) diffraction peak of the WNCF shifted towards a lower angle (∼22.6°) compared to WCF (∼24.4°), suggesting the presence of a larger interlayer spacing in WNCF due to the doping of heteroatom [32,33]. Raman spectroscopy was performed to further understand the structure information of carbon-based materials. In the Raman spectra of WCF (c) and WNCF (d) (Fig. 1D), two intense bands of D band at ∼1350 cm−1 and G band at ∼1595 cm−1 were related to the structure defects of the disordered carbon and E
2g in-plane vibration of sp
2 C atom, respectively [34,35]. Therefore, the relative intensity ratio of D and G bands (I
D
/I
G ) can be employed as an index for reflecting the defect degrees of the carbon-based materials [26,34]. The I
D
/I
G value of the WNCF (2.1) was higher than that of the WCF (1.27), suggesting that nitrogen doping would lead to higher density of edge-plane-like defective sites (EDSs) existing on the surface of WNCF compared to WCF.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were also carried out to acquire the elemental compositions of the WCF and WNCF. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the XPS spectra showed that the WCF (a) only contained C (∼284.5 eV) and O (∼531.0 eV) elements, whereas WNCF (b) contained C (∼285.6 eV), N (∼398.7 eV) and O (∼531.9 eV) elements, implying that nitrogen element has been successfully doped into the carbon materials by using the green reagent of natural milk [36]. The relative element percentage of C, N and O was 84.43 at.%, 2.49 at.% and 13.08 at.% for WNCF, respectively, while the percentage of C and O was 90.36 at.% and 9.64 at.% for WCF, respectively. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum of WNCF (Fig. 2B) can be deconvoluted into three different peaks arising from: pyridinic N (∼398.0 eV), pyrrolic N (∼400.3 eV) and graphitic N (∼401.1 eV) [34,37]. In addition, the high-resolution O1s spectra of WCF and WNCF were shown in Fig. 2C, in which the deconvoluted three peaks at ∼530.9, ∼532.4 and ∼533.7 eV can be assigned to COOH/C–O, C–O and –OH groups, respectively [38,39]. Fig. 2D displayed the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of WCF (c) and WNCF (d). As shown, the absorption bands at ∼1639 and ∼1072 cm−1 were ascribed to CO and C–O stretching vibrations, respectively [40–42]. Additionally, the absorption peaks at ∼2958 and ∼3420 cm−1 were assigned to the C–H and –OH stretching vibrations [43–45]. Notably, the peak recorded for WNCF at ∼1389 cm−1 was attributed to the -N-H stretching vibration [46], which cannot be found for WCF. Therefore, the FT-IR analysis further provided evidence for the existence of O, N and C elements on the WNCF.

The electrochemical characteristics of different electrodes were first investigated in 10 mM K3Fe(CN)6 containing 0.1 M KCl by cyclic voltammetry (CV). As shown in Fig. 3 A, a well-defined CV curve with almost complementary anodic and cathodic peaks was observed at bare GCE (a1). However, the original redox peaks almost disappeared at Nafion modified GCE (Nafion/GCE, a2), which could be attributed to the fact that the negative surface charge of Nafion hindered the diffusion behavior of Fe(CN)6 3− into the electrode and caused poor electron transfer [28]. However, compared to Nafion/GCE, higher peaks currents were obtained at the Nafion-dispersed WCF (Nafion-WCF) modified GCE (Nafion-WCF/GCE, a3) and Nafion-dispersed WNCF (Nafion-WNCF) modified GCE (Nafion-WNCF/GCE, a4). Among them, Nafion-WNCF/GCE (a4) exhibits more favorable electrochemical activity with more obvious peak current increase than that observed at Nafion-WCF/GCE (a3), probably because the nitrogen doping in the WNCF is capable of facilitating electron transfer [47]. The enhanced electron-transfer property of Nafion-WNCF/GCE was further verified by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis. Fig. 3B showed the Nyquist curves of GCE (b1), Nafion/GCE (b2), Nafion-WCF/GCE (b3) and Nafion-WNCF/GCE (b4) in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.1 M KCl. It can be found that the charge transfer resistance (R
ct ) of GCE (347 Ω) was lower than that of Nafion/GCE (3598 Ω), suggesting that the negatively charged Nafion was adverse for the diffusion of Fe(CN)6 3−/4-, causing a poor electron transfer behavior [48]. Compared to Nafion/GCE (3598 Ω) and Nafion-WCF/GCE (2080 Ω), Nafion-WNCF/GCE (1022 Ω) exhibited the lowest R
ct value, indicating that nitrogen doping makes the electron transfer rate faster.

The analytical characteristics of Nafion modified bismuth film-based GCE (Nafion/BFGCE), Nafion-WCF modified BFGCE (Nafion-WCF/BFGCE) and Nafion-WNCF modified BFGCE (Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE) for the stripping of 50 μg L−1 lead (II) (Pb2+) were investigated by the method of differential pulse anodic stripping voltammetry (DPASV) in 0.1 M pH 4.5 acetate buffer. All bismuth film electrodes displayed well-defined DPASV responses for Pb2+ due to the formation of fused alloys between lead and bismuth resulting in the easy reduction of Pb2+ [48] (Fig. 3C). Compared to Nafion/BFGCE (c1), the stripping current signalof Pb2+ at Nafion-WCF/BFGCE (c2) was only slightly improved. However, Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE (c3) took 214.5% current response enhancement compared to Nafion/BFGCE. Overall, Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE showed higher stripping performance for Pb2+ than that of Nafion-WCF/BFGCE. This result should be ascribed to the nitrogen doping on WNCF, since the introduction of nitrogen endows high density of defective sites, providing abundant effective sites for the nucleation lead and bismuth during the electrodeposition process [26–28]. In other words, Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE provided an advanced and high-performance electrochemical sensing platform for stripping of Pb2+. So we selected Nafion-WNCF as the electrode material for the detection of Pb2+ in the present work.

To acquire optimal performance for Pb2+ detection at Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE, some key electrochemical parameters (such as calcination temperature of WNCF, pH value of buffer solution, deposition potential, deposition time and bismuth ion (Bi3+) concentration) were investigated. The effect of the calcination temperature during WNCF synthesis on Pb2+ stripping was firstly evaluated. In addition to the carbonization temperature of 800 °C, we synthesized the WNCF at the carbonization temperatures of 700 °C and 900 °C as well (denoted as WNCF-700 and WNCF-900, respectively). The DPASV responses of the three materials-modified electrodes toward Pb2+ detection were evaluated. As observed in Fig. 4 A, Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE showed the highest DPASV response for Pb2+detection compared to Nafion-dispersed WNCF-700 modified BFGCE (Nafion-WNCF-700/BFGCE) and Nafion-dispersed WNCF-900 modified BFGCE (Nafion-WNCF-900/BFGCE). So the optimal carbonization temperature of 800 °C was chosen in subsequent experiments.

As depicted in Fig. 4B, the DPASV responses of Pb2+ at Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE showed an increasing tendency as the pH value increased from 3.0 to 4.5 and reached a maximum at pH 4.5. This can be explained that the high concentration of H+ and the heavy metal ions compete intensely for the binding sites of WNCF in an acidic pH condition (lower than 4.5). Additionally, the decreased DPASV responses of Pb2+ might be attributed to the excessive hydrolysis of Pb2+ at high pH (higher than 4.5), producing different forms of lead ions (Pb(OH)+, Pb(OH)2 0 and Pb(OH)3 −) [49]. Therefore, we chose pH 4.5 as the optimum pH to implement all the following experiments.

The deposition potential in DPASV test is closely correlated with the sensitivity of Pb2+ detection [50]. Thus, the influence of the deposition potential on the DPASV responses of Pb2+ at Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE was investigated from −0.9 to −1.4 V in 0.1 M pH 4.5 acetate buffer solution. As shown in Fig. 4C, when the potential shifted from −0.9 to −1.2 V, the DPASV responses of Pb2+ increased notably. However, as the deposition potential further negatively shifted to −1.4 V, the DPASV responses of Pb2+ showed a gradually decreasing trend. It can be explained that the produced hydrogen bubbles at a more negative potential attach to the electrode surface, resulting in reduced contact area between Pb2+ and the working electrode [51]. Therefore, the optimal deposition potential of −1.2 V was chosen in subsequent experiments.

Since the deposition time exerts a great influence on the detection limit and sensitivity of the designed electrochemical sensor [52], the effect of different deposition time varying from 60 to 480 s on the DPASV responses for Pb2+ detection was investigated at Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE. As shown in Fig. 4D, the increase of DPASV responses of Pb2+ could be observed with the deposition time varying from 60 to 360 s. However, the responses slightly decreased with further prolonging the deposition time up to 480 s, which is attributed to the fouling of electrode surface caused by excessive deposition of heavy metals after longer deposition time [53]. Therefore, a deposition time of 360 s was selected for all subsequent experiments.

The Bi3+ concentration controls the thickness of the bismuth film, so it shows a considerable effect on DPASV response [28]. In Fig. 4E, we investigated the dependence of the DPASV responses of Pb2+ on the Bi3+ concentrations (0.2–2.2 μg mL−1) in 0.1 M pH 4.5 acetate buffer solution. As shown in Fig. 4E, the DPASV responses of Pb2+ increased rapidly with increasing Bi3+ concentrations from 0.2 to 1.4 μg mL−1 and achieved a maximum value at 1.4 μg mL−1. However, the responses gradually decreased when Bi3+ concentration was higher than 1.4 μg mL−1, probably because the increased bismuth film thickness was adverse for mass transfer of Pb2+ during the stripping step [54]. Thus, an optimum Bi3+ concentration of 1.4 μg mL−1 was selected in the all subsequent measurements.

Overall, the optimal conditions including a carbonization temperature of 800 °C, a pH 4.5 of acetate buffer solution, a deposition time of 360 s, a Bi3+ concentration of 1.4 μg mL−1 and a deposition potential of −1.2 V, were chosen for subsequent DPASV experiments to en sure the highest DPASV response for Pb2+ detection.

The tolerance ratio experiments were carried out to evaluate the possible influence of coexisting metal ions with Pb2+ in real water samples. The tolerance value of Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE was prescribed that the peak signals keep relative error of ±5% with the DPASV responses of 50 μg L−1 Pb2+ in the existence of interfering species. In Fig. 5C, it was obvious that a 300-fold Fe2+, 70-fold Ca2+, 50-fold Ni2+, K+, Na+ and Hg2+, 10-fold Mg2+ and Zn2+, 5-fold Co2+, Mn2+ and 1-fold mass ratio of Cd2+, Al3+ and Fe3+ were found to be able to satisfy the tolerance ratio for the detection of 50 μg L−1 Pb2+ at Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE. The results suggested that most of the coexisting metal ions bring about no significant interference for Pb2+ detection. However, the DPASV response reduced upon the addition of 1-fold Cu2+. Such behavior was always observed at bismuth-based electrochemical Pb2+ sensors which is probably due to the presence of competition between copper and bismuth for surface sites of Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE [1,62]. However, when the samples contained high concentration of Cu2+, the addition of ferricyanide or Zn2+ would reduce the interference effect of Cu2+ [63].

The repeatability of the sensor is strongly related to the low-cost and routine monitoring of Pb2+ [63]. To evaluate the repeatability of Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE, ten repeated measurements for 50 μg L−1 Pb2+ in 0.1 M pH 4.5 acetate buffer solution were carried out. Acceptable relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.8% was obtained, which indicates the high repeatability of Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE. The reproducibility was further investigated by measuring the DPASV responses of 50 μg L−1 Pb2+ at five Nafion-WNCF/BFGCEs (Fig. 5D). The DPASV responses of Pb2+ at Nafion-WNCF/BFGCEs showed no significant changes with the RSD of 1.3%, revealing a reliable reproducibility. Additionally, the DPASV response of 50 μg L−1 Pb2+ at Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE remained more than 95% of its original value after four weeks of storage at 4 °C, indicating an excellent stability.

The detections of Pb2+ in real samples of tap water from the laboratory and lake water from the campus were performed to evaluate the practical application of Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE by employing the standard addition method. Fig. 5E–F presents the DPASV curves of real water samples with different spiked Pb2+ concentrations of 0, 10, 20 and 30 μg L−1 And the determined Pb2+ concentrations and the recoveries are summarized in Table 2 . The recoveries at Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE were found to be between 100.1% and 103.2%, suggesting that the highly practical potential of Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE for Pb2+ analysis in real samples [64,65]. To validate the precision of this method, our detection results were compared to the results obtained by graphite furnace atomic absorption spectrometry (GF-AAS). As shown in Table 2, the Pb2+ concentrations obtained by our electrochemical sensor were in good agreement with the results by using the GF-AAS method, indicating the reliability of Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE for Pb2+ determination in real samples.

The WNCF with abundant edge-plane-like defective sites was synthesized for the stripping analysis and detection of Pb2+ by using the biomasses of milk and wax gourd as the main nitrogen and carbon sources, respectively. The fabricated Pb2+ sensor based on Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE exhibits attractive analytical performance with a low detection limit and wide linear range. More importantly, the practical application of Nafion-WNCF/BFGCE is realized for Pb2+ measurement in tap water and lake water samples. The Pb2+ concentrations obtained from our system are consistent with those from GF-AAS, which indicates the great potential of green and low-priced nanomaterial of WNCF for the practical and reliable Pb2+ analysis in a wide range of water samples.

Cuixing Xu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Data curation, Validation. Jingju Liu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Data curation, Validation. Yanni Bi: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Data curation, Validation. Chongbo Ma: Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Jing Bai: Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Zongqian Hu: Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Ming Zhou: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Formal analysis, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21605015, 81301345 and 81871506), the Development Project of Science and Technology of Jilin Province (20170101176JC), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (JGPY201802), the Jilin Provincial Department of Education, and Analysis and Testing Center of Northeast Normal University.
